articles.full_text:
Monsoon likely in two days:Rain, thunderstorm alert in 30 UP districts, temperatures to drop by 8°C
Lucknow/Varanasi/Prayagraj/Kanpur8 hours ago
The picture is from Kanpur Dehat, where light rain on Friday brought relief from the scorching heat.
Monsoon continues to elude Uttar Pradesh, with its arrival delayed by nearly two weeks. The southwest monsoon has remained stalled near Maharajganj on the Uttar Pradesh–Bihar border for the past 15 days. It usually enters the state by June 20, but this year it is now expected to arrive around June 29, nearly eight days behind schedule.
Most parts of the state woke up to bright sunshine on Saturday morning. Mild winds kept temperatures slightly lower in the early hours, but the heat is expected to intensify through the day. Several cities are likely to record temperatures above 42°C, while hot winds will add to people's discomfort.
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a thunderstorm and rain alert for 30 districts, with lightning expected at isolated places. A heatwave warning remains in place for 22 districts. On Friday, Lakhimpur Kheri was the hottest city in the state at 43.2°C, followed by Banda and Agra (43°C each), Lucknow (42.4°C), Aligarh (42.2°C) and Prayagraj (42°C).
The weather department said monsoon showers are likely to begin in Uttar Pradesh within the next two days. Even areas that do not receive rain immediately are expected to witness increased cloud cover. Temperatures are likely to fall by 8–9°C, bringing relief from the scorching heat and heatwave conditions. Widespread rainfall is expected across most parts of the state from June 30.
'Conditions favourable for monsoon to enter UP in two days'
Senior scientist Atul Kumar Singh of the Lucknow Meteorological Centre said, "The southwest monsoon has remained stalled near Maharajganj for the past 15 days. Conditions are now becoming favourable for its advance into Uttar Pradesh over the next two days. Heatwave conditions will continue in parts of the state today and tomorrow. However, eastern Uttar Pradesh, the Terai region and a few other areas may witness strong winds and light rainfall during this period."
Weather forecast for the next five days
- June 28: Heat will intensify in western Uttar Pradesh, while eastern parts may receive rain. Heatwave conditions are likely to persist.
- June 29: Light rain is likely in western Uttar Pradesh, while heavy rainfall is expected in eastern districts.
- June 30: Thunderstorms and rain are expected in western Uttar Pradesh, with strong winds and widespread rain in the east.
- July 1: Light showers are likely in western Uttar Pradesh, while eastern districts may witness thunderstorms and rain. Temperatures are expected to ease.
- July 2: Light rain is expected to begin across western Uttar Pradesh, while eastern districts may receive rain accompanied by thunderstorms.
Rainfall remains uneven across Uttar Pradesh
Rainfall has been uneven across the state's 75 districts this June. Thirty-four districts have recorded a large rainfall deficit, while 13 have received below-normal rain. Sixteen districts have reported normal rainfall, four have recorded above-normal rainfall and seven have received significantly more rainfall than usual. Kaushambi has not recorded any rainfall so far this month.
Why is the monsoon moving slowly?
According to the weather department, the weakening of the jet stream is delaying the advance of the southwest monsoon. Meteorologists expect it to move forward once atmospheric conditions improve.
UP may receive 8% below-normal rainfall this season
Senior scientist Atul Singh said Uttar Pradesh is likely to receive below-normal rainfall this monsoon season. The state usually records 820–840 mm of rainfall between June and September, but this year it is expected to receive only 754–773 mm, around 8% below normal.
El Niño signals raise concern
IMD Director General Dr M. Mahapatra said La Niña-like conditions over the Pacific Ocean are fading and signs of El Niño are emerging, which could reduce rainfall this year. Reduced snow cover in the Northern Hemisphere during January–March may also affect the strength of the southwest monsoon.
Impact on farming and food prices
Below-normal rainfall could affect Uttar Pradesh's economy, agriculture and daily life. Paddy production may decline by up to 20%, while delayed rainfall in June and July could postpone paddy transplantation and reduce the cultivated area.
Farmers may have to rely more on tube wells for irrigation, increasing electricity and diesel costs. Lower crop production could push up the prices of cereals, pulses and vegetables. Drought conditions may also deepen in Bundelkhand and parts of south-western Uttar Pradesh, including Jhansi, Mahoba, Lalitpur and Mathura.
Last year's monsoon was above normal
According to the IMD, Uttar Pradesh received an average of 870–900 mm of rainfall during the 2025 monsoon season (June to September), around 10–15% above the long-term average.
